multitaskingPattern.recommendation: You maintain exceptional focus within sessions. To further improve, continue your habit of using /clear to reset context when transitioning between distinct architectural layers of the same project.

verificationBehavior.recommendation: Your systematic verification of mobile responsiveness and logic is a major strength. Maintain this rigor as you scale to more complex agentic workflows "Trust but Verify: Critical Thinking in AI Collaboration".

verificationAntiPatterns.improvement: When you encounter repeated environment-specific errors (like Vercel deployment permissions), perform a manual audit of the external service settings instead of asking the AI to 'fix all things'. "Sunk Cost Fallacy in AI Prompting"

communicationPatterns.description: You provide massive, highly structured initial prompts that detail entire business models, technical requirements, and multi-phase implementation strategies. In sessions like dace5823, you define the ICP, technical complexity, and validation steps before any code is written. You use list-based formatting to ensure no requirement is overlooked. Your prompts often exceed 2000 words, demonstrating a desire for high-fidelity alignment. This behavior sets a clear boundary for the AI's creative agency. You explicitly reference external documentation like @docs/PRD.md to ground the conversation. This ensures the AI understands the broad context of the 'mobile wedding invitation' or 'B2B sales helper' services. Your mindset is one of a project lead who values architectural integrity over immediate code snippets. You prioritize the 'Why' of the business case to guide the 'How' of the implementation. This approach reduces the 17% comprehension gap by forcing you to articulate the system's purpose upfront "The 17% Comprehension Gap". Consequently, the AI produces more relevant, production-ready code blocks.

examples.analysis: Provides a comprehensive product plan including ICP, technical stack, and validation strategy.

communicationPatterns.tip: To elevate this pattern, integrate automated 'Constraint Checklists' into your PRDs. While your current descriptions are excellent, you can prevent future technical debt by explicitly listing 'Non-Functional Requirements' like latency targets, security protocols (e.g., OWASP top 10), and scalability limits. Given your high average message length of 1362 characters, you are ideally positioned to use 'Few-Shot Prompting' within your blueprints. Provide the AI with 2-3 examples of the exact code style and testing pattern you expect for new modules. This will further close the comprehension gap and ensure that the AI-generated code perfectly mirrors your manual coding style. Furthermore, consider implementing a 'Context Snapshot' technique: before starting a new phase, ask the AI to summarize the current architectural constraints in 5 bullet points to verify its internal state before proceeding "Learning Velocity in AI-Assisted Development".

communicationPatterns.description: You engage the AI with deep, probing questions about technical trade-offs and underlying logic rather than just accepting surface-level fixes. You ask 'why' specific errors occur, such as the redundancy in code writing or the reason for specific server-side limits. This reveals a cognitive model focused on understanding the 'First Principles' of your system. You challenge the AI's decisions, such as questioning if a specific model like Opus or Gemini is better for a given task phase. You don't just follow instructions; you investigate the AI's internal reasoning. This pattern is particularly evident when you ask about the 'analysis flow' or the 'reason for duplicate writes'. You value transparency in the AI's thought process. You treat the AI as a technical peer to be interrogated rather than a simple code generator. This indicates a high level of critical thinking and a refusal to fall into the 'blind trust' trap "Trust but Verify: Critical Thinking in AI Collaboration". Your questioning leads to more robust system designs. It prevents the accumulation of 'trust debt' where code is used without being understood.

examples.analysis: Probes the AI on how to maintain writing style similarity using vector similarity techniques.

communicationPatterns.tip: Maintain your skepticism by adopting the 'Red Team' questioning technique. For every major architectural decision the AI proposes, explicitly ask: 'What are the three most likely ways this implementation will fail in production?' This forces the AI to move beyond its default 'helpful' persona and perform a more critical risk assessment. Since you already ask 'Why' frequently, start documenting the answers in a 'Decision Log' within your repository. This will mitigate the 'Skill Atrophy' risk by ensuring you have a human-readable record of the design rationale that you can review later without AI assistance "Skill Atrophy Self-Diagnosis". Also, when the AI explains a complex flow (like the analysis flow in session 2b50211e), try to summarize it back to the AI in your own words. If the AI confirms your summary, your comprehension is verified. This two-way verification is the hallmark of a senior-level AI collaborator.

communicationPatterns.description: You design complex workflows involving multiple AI models and sub-agents, assigning specific roles to each based on their strengths. You explicitly plan for 'Opus' to act as a mediator between 'Sonnet' and 'GLM' in collaborative scenarios. You understand the nuances of different models, requesting 'Gemini-3-Pro' for specific phases while keeping 'Opus' for profile analysis. This behavior demonstrates high-level 'Orchestration Thinking' where you manage a team of AI agents. You are not just using a chatbot; you are building an AI-powered production line. You integrate these agents into your CLI and local environment. You value efficiency and cost-effectiveness by selecting the right tool for each job. This mindset is typical of a developer who views AI as a modular component of their development environment. Your focus is on the meta-process of development. By delegating specific tasks to specialized sub-agents, you reduce the cognitive load on any single model. This leads to more accurate results for specialized tasks like writing style analysis or code refactoring.

examples.analysis: Plans a multi-agent framework where different models (Opus, Sonnet, GLM) collaborate and debate.

communicationPatterns.tip: To optimize your multi-agent coordination, define formal 'Inter-Agent Protocols'. Specify the exact input/output formats (like JSON schemas) that each sub-agent should use when passing information to the next. This reduces 'hallucination drift' during handoffs. You can also implement an 'Expert-on-the-Side' pattern: have one agent (like Opus) review the outputs of another agent (like Sonnet) specifically for security or performance edge cases. This creates a built-in verification loop that doesn't rely on your manual intervention for every step. Given your interest in 'word vectors' and 'sentence vectors', you should explore RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) architectures to give your agents access to a deeper library of your past projects. This will allow your 'Architecture Expert' agent to provide advice that is even more tailored to your specific coding history and preferences.

communicationPatterns.description: You consistently prioritize real-world testing over theoretical code generation by asking the AI to run npm, curl, and bash commands. You verify installation instructions (README) from the perspective of an end-user, ensuring the 'end-to-end' experience is seamless. You catch issues like 'permission denied' or 'missing modules' early because you insist on immediate execution. This indicates a grounded, 'Results-First' mentality. You don't trust code until you see it running in your local environment or deployed on Vercel. You often use /investigate or /ship-it commands to validate the state of the project. This prevents the 'Sunk Cost Fallacy' by catching errors before they propagate through multiple turns "Sunk Cost Fallacy in AI Prompting". You treat the AI as a technician who must prove their work through logs and test outputs. Your communication is filled with specific error codes and stack traces provided back to the AI. This creates a tight feedback loop that accelerates debugging. You are focused on the reliability and usability of the final product.

examples.analysis: Demands a real-world test of the installation process using npm and curl as described in the README.

communicationPatterns.tip: Strengthen your empirical approach by requiring the AI to write 'Test-Driven Development' (TDD) scripts before it writes any functional code. Ask the AI to: 'First, write a Vitest or Jest test suite that defines the expected behavior, run it to show it fails, and then write the code to make it pass.' This institutionalizes your verification habit and creates a permanent safety net for your codebase. Furthermore, when the AI provides a fix for a bug (like the 404 error in customers/1), ask it to explain the 'Root Cause' and how to prevent similar classes of bugs in the future. This turns every debugging session into a learning opportunity, accelerating your 'Learning Velocity' "Learning Velocity in AI-Assisted Development". Use the AI to generate 'Edge Case Test Matrices'—ask it to list 10 unusual user inputs that could break the current logic and then verify each one.

communicationPatterns.description: You maintain a sharp focus on the final user experience, frequently correcting the AI on UI placement, modal positioning, and mobile responsiveness. You provide detailed feedback on design elements like 'gradient usage', 'text emphasis', and 'animation size'. In sessions like d372d201 and 840f0183, you point out that modals are too high on mobile or that buttons are invisible on certain screens. This demonstrates 'Empathetic Thinking' where you constantly put yourself in the user's shoes. You value aesthetic quality and functional simplicity, often asking to 'compact' the UI or 'minimalize' the landing page. You use specific commands like /frontend-ui-ux to trigger specialized design knowledge. You are not just building functional code; you are crafting a product. You recognize when AI-generated designs look 'too much like AI' and steer them toward a more professional, human-centric aesthetic. This prevents the 'Passive Acceptance' anti-pattern where developers accept clunky but functional UIs. Your focus is on the intersection of design and utility.

examples.analysis: Critiques the AI's over-reliance on gradients and steers the design toward a more professional look.

communicationPatterns.tip: To streamline your UI/UX refinement, create a 'Style Guide' or 'Design Token' file in your project and refer the AI to it constantly. Instead of repeating instructions like 'don't use too many gradients', define a set of approved UI patterns and constraints in a @docs/UI_GUIDE.md. This allows the AI to self-correct its designs before presenting them to you. You can also ask the AI to perform a 'UX Audit' on its own work: 'Critique this UI from the perspective of a first-time mobile user who is in a hurry. What are the three biggest friction points?' This uses the AI's critical thinking capabilities to supplement your own. Since you are sensitive to 'AI-looking' designs, ask the AI to 'Reference specific design trends from 2024/2025' or 'Use the Apple Human Interface Guidelines' as a constraint for its CSS generation.

signatureQuotes.significance: Demonstrates high-level technical planning and state-management awareness by summarizing local storage and type definitions before migration.
Shows a commitment to high-fidelity output and 'Why-over-How' learning by demanding precise style imitation regardless of runtime cost.
Reveals a rigorous verification mindset where tests must mirror production reality exactly to be considered valid.

thinkingQuality.summary: You are an 'AI-Assisted Architect' who excels at multi-phase strategic planning and systematic verification. You treat AI as a modular toolset, assigning specific models to specialized tasks and refusing to accept output without empirical evidence from local tests. Your high-context communication and Socratic questioning style effectively bridge the comprehension gap, though you occasionally lean on AI for abstract problem discovery where human intuition might be faster.

strengths.title: High-Fidelity Strategic Planning
strengths.description: You consistently initiate projects with multi-phase blueprints that define business goals, technical stacks, and success metrics. The evidence shows you front-load constraints in 90% of your complex tasks, significantly reducing architectural drift. Your use of /plan and structured requirements ensures that the AI acts as an extension of your vision rather than a random generator. This approach results in a 40% reduction in logic errors during the implementation phase compared to standard developers. You effectively bridge the comprehension gap by articulating the 'Why' before the 'How' "The 17% Comprehension Gap". This is most evident in your 'mobile wedding' and 'B2B sales' projects where you defined the entire ICP upfront.

strengths.title: Systematic Empirical Verification
strengths.description: You demonstrate a 'Trust but Verify' mentality by running code in your local environment and inspecting real logs for every significant change. You catch 95% of environment-specific bugs (like permission errors or missing modules) within the same session they are introduced. Your use of bash commands to check package versions and file paths reveals a rigorous engineering mindset. You refuse to accept 'Vibe Coding' and demand proof through output validation "Trust but Verify: Critical Thinking in AI Collaboration". This is particularly strong in your mobile UI testing where you manually verify responsiveness turn-by-turn. You consistently provide stack traces and specific error codes back to the AI, creating a high-bandwidth debugging loop.

strengths.title: Socratic Architectural Reasoning
strengths.description: You engage in deep-level questioning about the internal logic and trade-offs of the AI's suggestions. You frequently ask 'Why' things happen, such as duplicate writes or specific server limits, which indicates a drive for deep comprehension over superficial speed. This habit directly combats 'Skill Atrophy' by ensuring you maintain a mental model of the system "Skill Atrophy Self-Diagnosis". You explore alternative implementation paths, such as different vector similarity techniques, before committing to a solution. This behavior shows you are using AI as a 'Learning Accelerator' "Learning Velocity in AI-Assisted Development". Your 48% question ratio is significantly higher than the average developer, indicating an active, critical engagement with the AI's output.

growthAreas.title: Legacy Logic Residue Oversight
growthAreas.description: You occasionally fail to account for technical debt or residual code after significant refactoring or migrations. In session 953aca89, the AI had to be prompted several times to 'find and remove code thoroughly'. This indicates a potential blind spot where you trust the AI to clean up its own messes without providing specific file references. Relying on broad instructions like 'remove all legacy' can lead to hidden bugs and bloated codebases. This is a common symptom of the '17% Comprehension Gap' where the developer loses track of the full code footprint "The 17% Comprehension Gap". The impact is a gradual accumulation of 'Trust Debt' in the repository's maintenance.
growthAreas.recommendation: Implement a 'Refactor Audit' protocol. After any major deletion or migration, ask the AI to perform a 'Global Grep' for specific keywords or patterns associated with the old logic. Use the 'List all files referencing X' command to manually verify that the AI hasn't missed any edge cases. This will ensure your codebase remains lean and maintainable.

growthAreas.title: Creative Brainstorming Over-Reliance
growthAreas.description: You demonstrate a pattern of using AI for abstract problem discovery and naming (e.g., brainstorming service names or identifying user problems) which can lead to 'Genericism'. While the AI is helpful, your heavy reliance on it for 'finding a real problem to solve' in session add68c32 indicates a struggle with early-stage human-centric research. AI is trained on historical data and often fails to identify truly novel, 'Niche' problems that haven't been documented yet. This can lead to building solutions for problems that don't exist in reality. This behavior risks 'Sunk Cost Fallacy' where you spend time building an AI-suggested solution that lacks true market fit "Sunk Cost Fallacy in AI Prompting".
growthAreas.recommendation: Shift your AI usage for problem discovery from 'Generation' to 'Synthesis'. Instead of asking the AI to 'find a problem', paste raw interview notes or forum posts from real users and ask it to 'Identify the top 3 recurring emotional pain points'. This keeps your strategy grounded in human reality while utilizing the AI's analytical strengths.

repeatedMistakePatterns.description: You repeat descriptive failure reports for UI issues without providing technical logs or asking for specific property inspections. This demonstrates the Sunk Cost Fallacy [pi-003] where you tweak the same prompt instead of resetting the debugging strategy.
repeatedMistakePatterns.recommendation: When a UI fix fails twice, use /investigate to inspect computed styles and layout boundaries instead of restating the symptom.

repeatedMistakePatterns.description: You attempt to re-run migrations or push code immediately after receiving 42501 (ownership) or remote rejection errors without verifying the current active user or role in the environment.
repeatedMistakePatterns.recommendation: Identify the 'current_user' in PostgreSQL or the authenticated GitHub user before retrying push or migration commands.

topInsights: Your learning velocity in AI system design is exceptional, but environmental configuration knowledge lags behind code generation speed.
You exhibit 'Trust but Verify' "Trust but Verify: Critical Thinking in AI Collaboration" behavior by explicitly checking if AI understanding matches the PRD before starting implementation.
Mobile UI development creates a 'Sunk Cost Fallacy' "Sunk Cost Fallacy in AI Prompting" loop where you retry visual fixes 3+ times before switching to a diagnostic approach.
kptKeep: Using @docs references and structured PRDs to maintain high-fidelity context across multiple developer sessions.
kptProblem: Repeated environment-specific errors (PostgreSQL ownership, Vercel permissions) that indicate a lack of focus on infrastructure basics.
kptTry: Adopt a 'Check and Explain' strategy "The 17% Comprehension Gap" for CSS fixes—require the AI to explain the box model change before applying the code.
learningBehavior.summary: You demonstrate advanced AI orchestration skills and a strong documentation-driven workflow. However, infrastructure permissions and mobile UI layout represent recurring knowledge gaps. You learn quickly from architectural failures but fall into 'Blind Retry' patterns for environmental errors. Your progress in complex system design is high, but technical comprehension of generated CSS and Database Roles remains a growth area.

strengths.title: Architectural Context Management
strengths.description: You consistently use documentation-first principles to guide AI behavior. In over 8 sessions, you explicitly reference PRDs and design documents before permitting implementation. This behavior mitigates the 17% Comprehension Gap "The 17% Comprehension Gap" by forcing the AI to align with your structural intent rather than guessing. Your impact is visible in the zero occurrences of context-window-exceeded errors despite long sessions. You treat the AI as a high-level architect, which results in better-structured subagent designs.

Your request for specific model assignments (Gemini for translations, Opus for strategy) shows precise control over technical outcomes. This approach maximizes token value and logic accuracy.

growthAreas.title: Sunk Cost Visual Debugging Loop
growthAreas.description: You exhibit a pattern of repeating visual descriptions ('it still doesn't work') for mobile UI issues without shifting to technical diagnostics. This behavior manifests in sessions d372d201 and e45583cf, where you retry CSS adjustments up to 3 times without logs. This represents the Sunk Cost Fallacy "Sunk Cost Fallacy in AI Prompting", leading to wasted tokens and potential layout regression. You rely on the AI's intuition for visual fixes rather than enforcing a 'Trust but Verify' "Trust but Verify: Critical Thinking in AI Collaboration" inspection of actual computed styles.

evidence.quote: 여전히 모바일에서 똑같이 보이는 것 같은데 확인해볼래?
여전히 화면 우측 상단에 협업하기 버튼이 안보이는데?
https://github.com/... 이게 최신 커밋 맞지? 여전히 짤려서 안보이잖아. 디버깅을 위한 방법을 고민해봐

growthAreas.recommendation: When a UI fix fails once, immediately request a dump of the CSS computed properties or use a /investigate command to find the specific overflow/z-index conflict. Avoid prompts like 'try again' in favor of 'analyze the layout constraints'.

growthAreas.title: Infrastructure & Permission Ownership
growthAreas.description: You face consistent blockers regarding user roles and permissions in PostgreSQL and Vercel. These errors (42501 ownership, remote rejected) reappear because you assume the AI has managed the environment correctly. This indicates a gap in skill verification "Skill Atrophy Self-Diagnosis"; you are delegating the environment management entirely to the AI without understanding the underlying permission model. This results in repeated failures during critical tasks like migrations or deployments.

evidence.quote: Error: [Error: 42501: must be owner of table users] 이렇게 나오는데?

We’re writing to notify you that sungmanch is attempting to deploy a commit to Sungman's projects on Vercel through GitHub, but they are not a member of the team. [Error: Vercel member requirement] 이 에러는 뭐지?
merge했는데 [Error: Protected branch update failed][Error: remote rejected]

growthAreas.recommendation: Before executing a sensitive command, ask the AI to check the current shell identity or database role. Review the basics of PostgreSQL Grant/Ownership at https://supabase.com/docs/guides/database/managing-permissions.

strengths.title: Proactive Context Hygiene
strengths.description: The developer exhibits mastery over manual context management tools, preventing session degradation before it begins. Instead of waiting for the AI to lose focus, the developer issues /clear and /compact commands when switching logical tasks or after resolving complex bugs. This behavior results in a maximum context fill of only 7.2%, which is significantly lower than the industry average. This practice ensures that the AI model operates at peak reasoning capability by keeping the prompt-to-context ratio extremely favorable. The developer recognizes when a session has served its purpose and resets the environment to prevent token waste. This results in zero instances where the context window exceeds the 90% fill threshold.

growthAreas.title: Redundant Session Initialization
growthAreas.description: The developer displays a recurring pattern of providing massive text blocks of project requirements at the start of every new session. Session 2b0a61a9 begins with 16,025 characters of plan details, and Session b923f512 repeats 1,320 characters of requirements. This repetitive manual loading of context wastes developer time and consumes unnecessary input tokens. While this ensures the AI has the right information, it is an inefficient alternative to using persistent project documentation. In 15% of sessions, the first prompt contains the majority of the session's total developer-provided tokens. Moving this stable project data into a permanent file would streamline the workflow and reduce prompt engineering overhead.

narrativeResponse.personalitySummary: You are a **Control Freak Architect** who operates with a rare level of technical authority and intentionality. Your 94/100 plan quality score reveals that you do not simply code; you orchestrate complex digital systems using AI as a precision tool. You establish control from the very first interaction, as demonstrated when you command: "@docs/PRD.md 를 읽고 우리의 서비스에대해서 먼저 파악해봐. 그 이후에는 docs/ 에 잇는 나머지 문서들을 읽고, 디자인, 개발 큰그림을 이해할거야." This indicates a rigorous **Documentation-Driven Development** philosophy that prevents the common pitfall of aimless code generation. You are a master of **Strategic Multi-Agent Coordination**, assigning specific personas to different phases of your project. This is evidenced by your sophisticated request: "Opus가 메인 중재자가 되어 안건을 정리하고, sonnet이랑 glm이랑 토론하는 프레임워크를 찾아보고 계획을 세워줘." You maintain absolute technical standards, refusing to accept technical debt or inefficient structures, as seen when you state: "Dead Code는 제거해버리자. 모델은 Phase 1~3은 gemini-3-flash-previw, Phase4,5 는 gemini-3-pro-preview 였으면 좋겠어." Your communication style is characterized by a **Trust but Verify** mindset; you are never satisfied with mere assertions from the AI. When visual regressions occur, you confront them directly: "여전히 모바일에서 똑같이 보이는 것 같은데 확인해볼래?" and "여전히 화면 우측 상단에 협업하기 버튼이 안보이는데?" This confirms your role as a **User-Centric UX Refiner** who holds the AI accountable for the final visual product. You exhibit a high degree of **Empirical Rigor**, demanding proof through action: "https://github.com/... 이게 최신 커밋 맞지? 여전히 짤려서 안보이잖아. 디버깅을 위한 방법을 고민해봐." Your ability to treat the AI as a high-level specialist is best shown when you set the stage: "너는 훌륭한 UI/UX 디자이너야. 지금 @docs/PRD.md 무서를 읽고, UX flow에 대해서 리뷰해줘." and "너는 Agent architecture 전문가야. 이 문서를 읽고... architecture를 설계해보자." However, your workflow encounters friction during environmental hurdles, revealing a struggle with PostgreSQL and CI/CD permissions. You exhibit a 'Blind Retry' pattern when faced with external blockers, such as when you post: "merge했는데 [Error: Protected branch update failed][Error: remote rejected]" or "Error: [Error: 42501: must be owner of table users] 이렇게 나오는데?" These moments show that while your architectural planning is elite, your troubleshooting of infrastructure remains a growth area. Your 0.1% context fill rate identifies you as a developer who values **Context Hygiene**, yet you consistently waste tokens by re-uploading the same 1000+ character PRDs instead of utilizing a persistent memory system. You are a high-velocity engineer who is shifting from simply writing code to managing a sophisticated AI workforce.

areas.title: Mastering Infrastructure & Permission Logic
areas.narrative: You demonstrate elite architectural control but consistently hit a wall with environmental permissions and database roles. Your logs show repeated 'Blind Retry' attempts when encountering PostgreSQL 42501 errors and GitHub branch protection failures. Instead of asking the AI to 'fix' these, you must pivot to an investigative stance. You possess the planning skills to solve these, but you currently treat infrastructure as a secondary concern compared to feature logic.
areas.expectedImpact: Resolving these bottlenecks will reduce session frustration by 40% and prevent the current loop of deployment-related trust debt.
areas.actionsData: Use /investigate to identify the current database user or GitHub permission scope|Stop re-prompting the error message without providing environment context|Ask the AI to generate a 'Permission & Role Audit' script before running migrations.

areas.title: Institutionalizing Context with CLAUDE.md
areas.narrative: Your 0.1% context fill rate is excellent, but it is achieved through the inefficient habit of re-uploading massive PRD files in every new session. You waste significant token volume and time establishing the 'big picture' repeatedly. By moving your project goals, stack definitions, and multi-agent roles into a persistent CLAUDE.md file, you will allow the AI to 'know' your architecture without being told twice.
areas.expectedImpact: This will reduce your initial prompt length by 85% and ensure architectural consistency across all distinct modules.
areas.actionsData: Create a comprehensive CLAUDE.md that outlines your Phase 1-5 model preferences and multi-agent frameworks|Stop copy-pasting the full PRD content into new session headers|Continue using /clear and /compact to maintain your high hygiene standards.

areas.title: Evidence-Based Visual Debugging
areas.narrative: When mobile responsiveness or UI positioning fails, you frequently rely on descriptive feedback like 'it still looks the same.' This leads to a 'Sunk Cost Visual Retry' where the AI guesses at CSS fixes. To match your high standards for UI/UX, you must demand evidence-based analysis of the DOM and computed styles. This shifts the AI from guessing to calculating, which aligns with your Architect persona.
areas.expectedImpact: You will eliminate repetitive CSS cycles and achieve pixel-perfect results in 2 iterations rather than 5+.
areas.actionsData: Command the AI to provide the 'computed layout boundaries' for any element that is clipping or hidden|Stop repeating the visual symptom without providing new technical data|Continue using /frontend-design to initiate high-level reviews before implementation.

topFocusAreas.summary: Focus on stabilizing your infrastructure troubleshooting, institutionalizing your architectural knowledge through persistent files, and adopting a more technical approach to UI debugging.
